title: Motivation in Practice: Job Design & Rewards
simple_html:
The previous lesson explained why people are motivated. This one is about the practical levers managers actually pull: how to design jobs and rewards so that motivation theory turns into real engagement and performance.

Designing the job itself

The biggest insight is that the work itself motivates. Boring, repetitive jobs drain people; rich jobs energize them. The Job Characteristics Model (JCM) says five features make work motivating: skill variety, task identity (doing a whole piece of work), task significance (it matters to others), autonomy (freedom to decide), and feedback. Autonomy and feedback matter most.

Job crafting

Employees don't just receive jobs — they can reshape them. Job crafting means proactively adjusting your tasks, relationships, or how you think about your work to make it more meaningful.

Rewards and reinforcement

OB Mod applies behavior science: reward the behaviors you want and they recur. But watch out — paying people for tasks they already enjoy can actually reduce their inner motivation.

Key takeaways

  • The work itself is a powerful motivator; enrich jobs, don't just enlarge them.
  • The JCM's five characteristics — variety, identity, significance, autonomy, feedback — drive engagement.
  • Employees can craft their own jobs to add meaning.
  • Reinforcement works, but extrinsic rewards can crowd out intrinsic motivation.
  • Match rewards to what employees actually value.
intermediate_html:
This lesson translates motivation theory into managerial practice through two main levers: job design (structuring the work to be intrinsically motivating) and reward systems (using reinforcement and incentives effectively). It is the applied counterpart to the theory lesson and a frequent exam topic.

The Job Characteristics Model

Hackman and Oldham's (1976) Job Characteristics Model (JCM) is the dominant framework. Five core job dimensions create three critical psychological states, which in turn produce motivational and performance outcomes:

Core dimensionPsychological state
Skill varietyExperienced meaningfulness
Task identity
Task significance
AutonomyExperienced responsibility
FeedbackKnowledge of results

These combine in the Motivating Potential Score: MPS = [(Variety + Identity + Significance) / 3] × Autonomy × Feedback. Because autonomy and feedback are multiplied, a job low on either has low motivating potential regardless of variety. Crucially, the relationship is moderated by Growth Need Strength — enriched jobs motivate most for people who want growth.

Approaches to job design

  • Job rotation — moving across tasks to reduce boredom and build skills.
  • Job enlargement — adding tasks at the same level (horizontal; often just "more of the same").
  • Job enrichment — adding responsibility and autonomy (vertical; the genuine JCM lever).

Job crafting

Wrzesniewski and Dutton (2001) introduced job crafting — the proactive, bottom-up reshaping of one's job. Employees craft tasks (what they do), relationships (whom they work with), and cognition (how they frame the job's purpose). Job crafting complements top-down design and boosts engagement and meaning.

Reinforcement and reward systems

OB Modification (OB Mod) applies operant conditioning (Skinner): identify, measure, analyze, intervene, and evaluate target behaviors using positive reinforcement, punishment, or extinction. Reinforcement schedules matter — variable-ratio schedules (rewarding after an unpredictable number of responses) produce the most persistent behavior. On rewards, designers choose among individual pay-for-performance (piece rate, merit, bonuses), gainsharing, profit-sharing, and skill-based pay. A key caution from self-determination theory: tangible rewards contingent on doing an interesting task can crowd out intrinsic motivation (the overjustification effect).

Worked example: redesigning a call-center role

Agents read scripts, escalate everything, and get no feedback — a low-MPS job (low autonomy, low feedback, low identity). Redesign: empower agents to resolve issues up to a dollar threshold (autonomy), let them own a customer from call to resolution (task identity), share customer-impact stories (significance), and give real-time satisfaction dashboards (feedback). Allow agents to craft their roles by specializing in problem types they enjoy. Replace a flat attendance bonus with recognition tied to resolution quality (avoiding crowding out). Predicted result: higher MPS, engagement, and first-call resolution, especially among high-growth-need agents.

How to apply it

  1. Audit jobs against the five JCM dimensions; prioritize autonomy and feedback.
  2. Enrich (vertical) rather than merely enlarge (horizontal).
  3. Enable job crafting alongside top-down redesign.
  4. Design rewards around what employees value, and protect intrinsic motivation.
full_html:
If motivation theory diagnoses the forces behind effort, this lesson supplies the design tools managers use to engineer those forces: job design and reward systems. It operationalizes Herzberg's motivators, goal-setting, expectancy, and self-determination theory into concrete interventions, and it is one of the most practically consequential topics in organizational behavior.

From job specialization to job enrichment

Classical management (Taylor, 1911) maximized efficiency through extreme specialization, but excessive simplification produces boredom, low quality, absenteeism, and turnover. Herzberg's (1968) call for vertical job loading — adding responsibility and control rather than mere tasks — distinguished genuine job enrichment (vertical) from job enlargement (horizontal, adding same-level tasks) and job rotation (periodic movement across tasks). Only enrichment reliably raises intrinsic motivation, because it increases the depth of responsibility, not just the breadth of activity.

The Job Characteristics Model

The field's central framework is Hackman and Oldham's (1976, 1980) Job Characteristics Model. Five core job dimensions — skill variety, task identity, task significance, autonomy, and feedback — generate three critical psychological states: experienced meaningfulness (from variety, identity, significance), experienced responsibility for outcomes (from autonomy), and knowledge of results (from feedback). These states yield outcomes including high intrinsic motivation, high-quality performance, satisfaction, and low absenteeism and turnover. The model quantifies enrichment through the Motivating Potential Score: MPS = [(Skill Variety + Task Identity + Task Significance) / 3] × Autonomy × Feedback. The multiplicative placement of autonomy and feedback means a deficiency in either drives MPS toward zero — empirically, autonomy and feedback are the strongest predictors. Two moderators matter: Growth Need Strength (enrichment benefits those high in growth needs most) and context satisfactions (pay, security, supervision). Meta-analyses broadly support links between core characteristics and satisfaction, with somewhat weaker links to objective performance (Fried & Ferris, 1987).

Job crafting: the bottom-up complement

Whereas the JCM is a top-down, manager-led approach, Wrzesniewski and Dutton's (2001) job crafting recognizes that employees proactively reshape their own jobs. They craft task boundaries (altering the type or number of tasks), relational boundaries (changing interactions), and cognitive boundaries (reframing the meaning and purpose of the work). Job crafting increases person-job fit, engagement, meaning, and resilience, and is now seen as an essential partner to formal redesign, especially in knowledge and remote work where managerial control is looser.

Reinforcement, OB Mod, and reward systems

The behaviorist tradition (Skinner, 1953) underlies OB Modification (Luthans & Kreitner, 1985), a five-step process — identify, measure, analyze (functional/ABC analysis), intervene, evaluate — that applies positive reinforcement, negative reinforcement, punishment, and extinction to shape behavior. Reinforcement schedules govern persistence: continuous reinforcement speeds learning, but intermittent schedules sustain behavior, with variable-ratio schedules (rewarding after an unpredictable number of responses) producing the highest, most extinction-resistant response rates. Reward-system design then chooses among individual incentives (piece rate, merit pay, bonuses, stock options), group/organization-wide plans (gainsharing à la Scanlon, profit-sharing, ESOPs), and skill- or competency-based pay. Expectancy theory (Vroom, 1964) reminds designers that incentives motivate only when effort-performance and performance-reward links are clear and the reward is valued (high valence).

The intrinsic-extrinsic tension

A central, well-documented debate concerns whether extrinsic rewards undermine intrinsic motivation. Deci's (1971) experiments and the meta-analysis by Deci, Koestner and Ryan (1999) show that tangible, expected rewards contingent on performing an intrinsically interesting task can reduce intrinsic motivation (the overjustification / cognitive-evaluation effect), because the locus of causality shifts from internal interest to external control. Self-determination theory (Deci & Ryan, 1985) frames the antidote: support autonomy, competence, and relatedness; use informational rather than controlling rewards; and reserve incentives for tasks that are not already intrinsically engaging. Cameron and Pierce (1994) offer a counterpoint, arguing the crowding-out effect is narrower than often claimed — the practical synthesis is to design rewards that signal competence and respect autonomy.

Worked example: calculating and improving MPS

A claims-processing role scores (1-7 scale): variety 3, identity 2, significance 3, autonomy 2, feedback 2. MPS = [(3+2+3)/3] × 2 × 2 = 2.67 × 4 = ~10.7 (on a scale up to 343) — very low. Redesign: bundle whole claims to one processor (identity → 5), let processors approve claims under a threshold (autonomy → 5), provide a dashboard of accuracy and customer outcomes (feedback → 5), and connect processors to customer-impact stories (significance → 5). New MPS = [(3+5+5)/3] × 5 × 5 = 4.33 × 25 ≈ 108 — a tenfold increase, concentrated in the high-leverage autonomy and feedback terms. Pair this with informational recognition rather than a controlling per-claim bonus to protect intrinsic motivation, and enable crafting for processors who prefer complex cases.

Nuances, debates, and common mistakes

  • Confusing enlargement with enrichment. Adding same-level tasks (horizontal) rarely motivates; depth (vertical) does.
  • Ignoring individual differences. Enrichment can stress low-growth-need employees; one design does not fit all.
  • Crowding out intrinsic motivation. Bolting cash bonuses onto already-meaningful work can backfire.
  • Rewarding the wrong behavior. "On the folly of rewarding A while hoping for B" (Kerr, 1975) — misaligned metrics produce gaming.
  • Neglecting fairness. Even well-designed incentives fail if employees perceive procedural or distributive injustice.

Cross-links

This lesson directly applies Motivation Theories (Lesson 3) — Herzberg's motivators, expectancy, goal-setting, and self-determination. Autonomy and meaning connect to leadership style (empowering and transformational leadership, Lesson 7) and to Organizational Culture (Lesson 9), since culture shapes how much autonomy and crafting are permitted. Reward fairness ties to equity and justice, anticipating Power, Politics & Conflict (Lesson 8).
